Items.Title: 立即停止使用 .env 文件！ | Stop Using .env Files Now!
Items.Description:
我曾在大型企业系统上工作过，我很失望地看到 99% 的 JavaScript 教程都告诉你使用.env进行生产配置和机密，这是愚蠢的。

是时候纠正这种困扰社区的行为了。 让我们探讨一下.env文件的问题以及最终的解决方案。

问题很好......这是一个文件。 您不应该将“生产”文件存储在您的存储库中； 你把它放在哪里？ 直接在虚拟机根目录？ 如果图像泄漏，每个人都可以访问您的秘密。

如果您需要更新数据库密码会怎样？ 更新.env的人将有权访问文件中的每个秘密。 我什至不必解释为什么这很糟糕。 每次必须更新配置时，更新它的人都可以看到每个秘密。

假设您正在部署一个需要更新配置/秘密变量的新功能，出现问题并且应用程序回滚。

哦，有人记得我们在.env中的最后一个值吗？ 我们没有历史记录，应用程序需要我们刚刚删除/覆盖的值。

配置服务器来救援🦸‍♂️

所有这些问题的完美解决方案是使用配置服务器。 配置服务器是用于存储配置和机密的外部化应用程序。 它被认为是跨环境管理机密的中心枢纽。

多个云服务可以作为我的开源爱好者的配置服务器，如AWS Parameter Store 、 Google Secrets Manager或HashiCorp Vault 。

一旦你创建了一个秘密或配置，大多数时候你会得到一个像https://app.com/config/DB_PASSWORD/v1这样的 URL。

让我们看看配置服务器如何解决.env文件的所有问题。

解决方法： 1. 存储.env文件

使用配置服务器，所有秘密都集中在一个地方，加密，并且只能由您批准的应用程序和用户访问。

解决： 2. 更新一个秘密

使用配置服务器，您无需更新密钥，而是创建一个新版本。 创建新版本后，您可以更新您的秘密 URL，例如.../config/DB_PASSWORD/v2或.../config/DB_PASSWORD/v3 。

解决：3.版本控制

与解决方案 2 一样，大多数配置服务器都有自动版本控制。 如果您需要更新密钥，请创建一个新版本并更新应用程序中的配置 URL。 如果应用程序被回滚，它将自动使用您最后一个配置 URL。

通过这种方式，机密得到保护，您的应用程序可以回滚，而以前的机密没有问题。

最后的想法💭

我希望现在开发人员将开始为他们的生产服务使用集中配置。 我不是说.env文件没有目的。 它们可用于本地或面向开发的环境。

我想说的是不要将有价值的秘密存储在简单的文件中，特别是如果我的数据在您的服务中。

关于作者👨🏾‍💻

我是 Gregory Gaines，一位简单的软件工程师 @Google，他正在努力写出好文章。 如果您想要更多内容，请在 Twitter 上关注我@GregoryAGaines 。

现在去创造一些伟大的东西！ 如果您有任何问题，请在 Twitter ( @GregoryAGaines ) 上联系我； 我们可以谈谈。

谢谢阅读！



原文:

I've worked on huge enterprise systems and I'm disappointed to see 99% of all JavaScript tutorials tell you to use a .env for production config and secrets which is dumb.

It's time to correct this behavior plaguing the community. Let's explore the problem with .env files and the ultimate solution.

The problem with .env files 🚫

1. Storing the .env file

The problem is well... It's a file. You are not supposed to store your "production" file in your repo; where do you put it? Directly in the VM root? What about Docker containers, do you bake your secrets directly in the image? If the image leaks, everyone has access to your secrets.

2. Updating a secret config

What happens if you need to update a database password? Whoever updates the .env will have access to every secret in the file. I don't even have to explain why this is bad. Every time a config has to be updated whoever is updating it can see EVERY secret.

3. Versioning

Let's say you are deploying a new feature that requires updating your config / secret variables, something goes wrong and the application gets rolled back.

Uh-oh does anyone remember the last values we had in the .env? We have no history and the application requires the values we just deleted/overwrote.

Config servers to the rescue 🦸‍♂️

The perfect solution to all these issues is using a config server. A config server is an externalized application for storing configs and secrets. It's considered the central hub for managing secrets across environments.

Multiple cloud services can function as a config server like AWS Parameter Store, Google Secrets Manager, or HashiCorp Vault for my open-source enthusiasts.

Once you create a secret or config, most of the time you are given a URL like https://app.com/config/DB_PASSWORD/v1.

Let's see how a config server solves all the issues with .env files.

Solving: 1. Storage the .env file

With a config server, all the secrets are centralized in one place, encrypted, and can only be accessed by applications and users you approve.

Solving: 2. Updating a secret

With a config server, instead of updating a secret, you create a new version. After creating a new version, you update your secret URL like .../config/DB_PASSWORD/v2 or .../config/DB_PASSWORD/v3.

Solving: 3. Versioning

Like with solution 2, most config servers have automatic versioning. If you need to update a secret, create a new version and update the config URL in the application. If the application gets rolled back, it will automatically use the last config URL you had in place.

This way secrets are protected and your applications can get rolled back with no issues with the previous secrets.

Final Thoughts 💭

I hope now developers will start using centralized configs for their production services. .env files are unreliable and have no access management, versioning, or safe updates.

I'm not saying .env file don't have a purpose. They can be used for local or development-oriented environments.

What I'm trying to say is don't store valuable secrets in simple files, especially if my data is in your service.

About the Author 👨🏾‍💻

I'm Gregory Gaines, a simple software engineer @Google who's trying to write good articles. If you want more content, follow me on Twitter at @GregoryAGaines.

Now go create something great! If you have any questions, hit me up on Twitter (@GregoryAGaines); we can talk about it.

Thanks for reading!

Items.Title: 无需编码即可在 Flask 中生成 API - 开源项目 | Generate APIs in Flask without coding - Open-Source Project
Items.Description:
你好编码员！

本文介绍了一个开源工具，能够使用Flask作为后端技术生成安全 API。 谢谢阅读！

可以在 YouTube 上找到演示该过程的视频材料（链接如下）。 以下是演讲稿：

  • 生成器是使用一种设计模式构建的，该模式将授权和助手等公共部分与与模型定义相关的固执部分分开。 该工具的功能组件如下所列：

    • 启动工具的 CLI 命令： flask gen_api
    • 模型解析器：加载定义
    • 配置加载器： API_GENERATOR部分
    • 像token_required这样的助手，在运行时检查权限
    • 使用上述所有信息并构建 API 的核心生成器

    为了使事情尽可能简单，流程始终是一种方式，API 服务在每次迭代时完全重新生成。

    对于好奇的人，该工具的主要部分将在下一节中详细介绍。 但是，要全面了解产品，可能需要对源代码进行逆向工程。

    模型的定义被隔离在apps/models.py文件中：

     # apps/models.py - Truncated content from apps import db class Book ( db . Model ): id = db . Column ( db . Integer , primary_key = True ) title = db . Column ( db . String ( 64 ))

    上述定义通知工具为Book模型生成 API。

    该工具通过gen_api调用，这是一个自定义命令，用于检查模型是否存在并执行核心生成器。 这是定义：

     # api_generator/commands.py - Truncated content def gen_api (): # Iterate on models defined in the App Config for model in API_GENERATOR . values (): # The Model DB existence is checked try : models = importlib . import_module ( "apps.models" ) ModelClass = getattr ( models , model ) ModelClass . query . all () except Exception as e : print ( f "Generation API failed: { str ( e ) } " ) return # All good, we can call the generator try : manager . generate_forms_file () manager . generate_routes_file () print ( "APIs have been generated successfully." ) except Exception as e : print ( f "Generation API failed: { str ( e ) } " )

    该模块为每个模型注入负载定义，并将数据注入具有预定义结构的模板文件，该结构提供 API 节点的通用结构：

    • 变异请求（创建、更新、删除）通过token_required装饰器进行保护

    这是用于为GET和CREATE请求生成单个模型（截断内容）的路由的模型相关服务节点框架。

     @ api . route ( '/{endpoint}/' , methods = [ 'POST' , 'GET' , 'DELETE' , 'PUT' ]) @ api . route ( '/{endpoint}/<int:model_id>/' , methods = [ 'GET' , 'DELETE' , 'PUT' ]) class { model_name } Route ( Resource ): def get ( self , model_id : int = None ): if model_id is None : all_objects = { model_name }. query . all () output = [{{ 'id' : obj . id , ** { form_name }( obj = obj ). data }} for obj in all_objects ] else : obj = { model_name }. query . get ( model_id ) if obj is None : return {{ 'message' : 'matching record not found' , 'success' : False }}, 404 output = {{ 'id' : obj . id , ** { form_name }( obj = obj ). data }} return {{ 'data' : output , 'success' : True }}, 200 ################################### ## Checked for permissions @ token_required def post ( self ): try : body_of_req = request . form if not body_of_req : raise Exception () except Exception : if len ( request . data ) > 0 : body_of_req = json . loads ( request . data ) else : body_of_req = {{}} form = { form_name }( MultiDict ( body_of_req )) if form . validate (): try : obj = { model_name }( ** body_of_req ) { model_name }. query . session . add ( obj ) { model_name }. query . session . commit () except Exception as e : return {{ 'message' : str ( e ), 'success' : False }}, 400 else : return {{ 'message' : form . errors , 'success' : False }}, 400 return {{ 'message' : 'record saved!' , 'success' : True }}, 200

    生成器使用Books模型作为输入提供的合成代码如下所示：

     @ api . route ( '/books/' , methods = [ 'POST' , 'GET' , 'DELETE' , 'PUT' ]) @ api . route ( '/books/<int:model_id>/' , methods = [ 'GET' , 'DELETE' , 'PUT' ]) class BookRoute ( Resource ): def get ( self , model_id : int = None ): if model_id is None : all_objects = Book . query . all () output = [{ 'id' : obj . id , ** BookForm ( obj = obj ). data } for obj in all_objects ] else : obj = Book . query . get ( model_id ) if obj is None : return { 'message' : 'matching record not found' , 'success' : False }, 404 output = { 'id' : obj . id , ** BookForm ( obj = obj ). data } return { 'data' : output , 'success' : True }, 200 @ token_required def post ( self ): try : body_of_req = request . form if not body_of_req : raise Exception () except Exception : if len ( request . data ) > 0 : body_of_req = json . loads ( request . data ) else : body_of_req = {} form = BookForm ( MultiDict ( body_of_req )) if form . validate (): try : obj = Book ( ** body_of_req ) Book . query . session . add ( obj ) Book . query . session . commit () except Exception as e : return { 'message' : str ( e ), 'success' : False }, 400 else : return { 'message' : form . errors , 'success' : False }, 400 return { 'message' : 'record saved!' , 'success' : True }, 200

    在这个API 生成的代码中，我们可以看到 Books 模型信息是使用通用模式注入到服务节点模板中的，而没有很多专门化。

    如前所述，变异请求由检查用户存在的token_required控制。 使用装饰器，开发人员可以控制访问，并且此版本中提供的基本检查可以轻松扩展到更复杂的检查，例如用户角色。

     def token_required ( func ): @ wraps ( func ) def decorated ( * args , ** kwargs ): if 'Authorization' in request . headers : token = request . headers [ 'Authorization' ] else : return { 'message' : 'Token is missing' , 'data' : None , 'success' : False }, 403 try : data = jwt . decode ( token , current_app . config [ 'SECRET_KEY' ], algorithms = [ "HS256" ]) current_user = Users . query . filter_by ( id = data [ 'user_id' ]). first () if current_user is None : return { 'message' : 'Invalid token' , 'data' : None , 'success' : False }, 403

    用户注册后，访问令牌由/login/jwt/路由根据注册的用户凭据（用户名和密码）提供。

    这个免费工具正在大力开发更多模式和功能，列在自述文件（产品路线图部分）中。 如果有人有功能请求，请随时使用 GitHub问题跟踪器提交 PR（产品请求）。

    • 谢谢阅读！ 如需更多资源和支持，请访问：

原文:

Hello coders!

This article presents an open-source tool able to generate secure APIs using Flask as backend technology. Soft UI Dashboard, the starter that incorporates the generator, is published on GitHub and based on the permissive (MIT) license, can be used in commercial projects or eLearning activities. Thanks for reading!

A video material that demonstrates the process can be found on YouTube (link below). Here is the transcript of the presentation:

  • ✅ Download the project using GIT
  • ✅ Start in Docker (the API is empty)
  • ✅ Define a Books model and generate the API
  • ✅ Access and interact with the API (CRUD calls)
  • ✅ Register a new model Cities
  • ✅ Re-generate the API using the CLI
  • ✅ Access and use the new API identity

✨ How it works

The Generator is built using a design pattern that separates the common part like authorization and helpers from the opinionated part that is tied to the model(s) definitions. The functional components of the tool are listed below:

  • CLI command that launches the tool: flask gen_api
  • Models parser: that loads the definitions
  • Configuration loader: API_GENERATOR section
  • Helpers like token_required, that check the permissions during runtime
  • The core generator that uses all the above info and builds API

To keep things as simple as possible, the flow is always one way, with the API service completely re-generated at each iteration.

For curious minds, the main parts of the tool are explained with a verbose presentation in the next sections. However, to understand in full the product, reverse engineering on the source code might be required.

👉 #1 - API Generator Input

The API service is generated based on the following input:

The model's definition is isolated in apps/models.py file:

# apps/models.py - Truncated content

from apps import db class Book(db.Model): id = db.Column(db.Integer, primary_key=True) title = db.Column(db.String(64))

The API_GENERATOR section specifies the models to be processed by the generator

API_GENERATOR = { "books": "Book",
}

The above definitions inform the tool to generate the API for the Book model.

👉 #2 - Custom CLI command

The tool is invoked via gen_api, a custom command that checks if the models exist and execute the core generator. Here is the definition:

# api_generator/commands.py - Truncated content 
def gen_api(): # Iterate on models defined in the App Config
 for model in API_GENERATOR.values(): # The Model DB existence is checked  try: models = importlib.import_module("apps.models") ModelClass = getattr(models, model) ModelClass.query.all() except Exception as e: print(f"Generation API failed: {str(e)}") return # All good, we can call the generator
 try: manager.generate_forms_file() manager.generate_routes_file() print("APIs have been generated successfully.") except Exception as e: print(f"Generation API failed: {str(e)}")

👉 #3 - Core API Generator

This module injects the loads the definition for each model and injects the data into template files with a predefined structure that provides a common structure of an API node:

  • GET requests are publically available (no authorization required)
  • Mutating requests (Create, Update, Delete) are protected via token_required decorator

Here is the model-dependent service node skeleton used to generate the routes for a single model (truncated content) for GET and CREATE requests.

@api.route('/{endpoint}/', methods=['POST', 'GET', 'DELETE', 'PUT'])
@api.route('/{endpoint}/<int:model_id>/', methods=['GET', 'DELETE', 'PUT'])
class {model_name}Route(Resource): def get(self, model_id: int = None): if model_id is None: all_objects = {model_name}.query.all() output = [{{'id': obj.id, **{form_name}(obj=obj).data}} for obj in all_objects] else: obj = {model_name}.query.get(model_id) if obj is None: return {{ 'message': 'matching record not found', 'success': False }}, 404 output = {{'id': obj.id, **{form_name}(obj=obj).data}} return {{ 'data': output, 'success': True }}, 200 ###################################
 ## Checked for permissions
 @token_required def post(self): try: body_of_req = request.form if not body_of_req: raise Exception() except Exception: if len(request.data) > 0: body_of_req = json.loads(request.data) else: body_of_req = {{}} form = {form_name}(MultiDict(body_of_req)) if form.validate(): try: obj = {model_name}(**body_of_req) {model_name}.query.session.add(obj) {model_name}.query.session.commit() except Exception as e: return {{ 'message': str(e), 'success': False }}, 400 else: return {{ 'message': form.errors, 'success': False }}, 400 return {{ 'message': 'record saved!', 'success': True }}, 200

The composition code provided by the generator using the Books model as input is shown below:

@api.route('/books/', methods=['POST', 'GET', 'DELETE', 'PUT'])
@api.route('/books/<int:model_id>/', methods=['GET', 'DELETE', 'PUT'])
class BookRoute(Resource): def get(self, model_id: int = None): if model_id is None: all_objects = Book.query.all() output = [{'id': obj.id, **BookForm(obj=obj).data} for obj in all_objects] else: obj = Book.query.get(model_id) if obj is None: return { 'message': 'matching record not found', 'success': False }, 404 output = {'id': obj.id, **BookForm(obj=obj).data} return { 'data': output, 'success': True }, 200 @token_required def post(self): try: body_of_req = request.form if not body_of_req: raise Exception() except Exception: if len(request.data) > 0: body_of_req = json.loads(request.data) else: body_of_req = {} form = BookForm(MultiDict(body_of_req)) if form.validate(): try: obj = Book(**body_of_req) Book.query.session.add(obj) Book.query.session.commit() except Exception as e: return { 'message': str(e), 'success': False }, 400 else: return { 'message': form.errors, 'success': False }, 400 return { 'message': 'record saved!', 'success': True }, 200

In this API-generated code, we can see the Books model information is injected into the service node template using generic patterns without many specializations.

As mentioned before, the mutating requests are controlled by the token_required that checks the user's existence. Using the decorator the developer controls the access and the basic check provided in this version can be easily extended to more complex checks like user roles.

def token_required(func): @wraps(func) def decorated(*args, **kwargs): if 'Authorization' in request.headers: token = request.headers['Authorization'] else: return { 'message': 'Token is missing', 'data': None, 'success': False }, 403 try: data = jwt.decode(token, current_app.config['SECRET_KEY'], algorithms=["HS256"]) current_user = Users.query.filter_by(id=data['user_id']).first() if current_user is None: return { 'message': 'Invalid token', 'data': None, 'success': False }, 403

Once the user is registered, the access token is provided by the /login/jwt/ route based on registered user credentials (username and password).

This free tool is under heavy development for more patterns and features, listed on the README (product roadmap section). In case anyone has a feature request, feel free to use the GitHub Issues tracker to submit a PR (product request).

  • ✅ Dynamic DataTables: Server-side pagination, Search, Export
  • ✅ Stripe Payments: One-Time and Subscriptions
  • ✅ Async Tasks: Celery and Redis

Thanks for reading! For more resources and support, please access:

开发人员

发表于欢迎来到开发！

  1. 在下方留言，介绍一下自己吧！ 你可以谈论是什么让你来到这里，你正在学习什么，或者只是关于你自己的一个有趣的事实。

  2. 回复某人的评论，无论是提出问题还是打个招呼。 很高兴有你在社区！

内尔明·卡拉潘季奇 - 一旦暂停，thepracticaldev 将无法评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

一旦解除暂停，thepracticaldev 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，thepracticaldev 的所有帖子都将被隐藏，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 practicaldev 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

Items.Title: 现代*无服务器*打字稿作业队列 >= 2022 | Modern *serverless* typescript job queues in >= 2022
Items.Description:
与 2022 年相比，我们更接近 2023 年 1 月。无服务器已经接管了我们的堆栈，但我们仍在为后台工作和计划​​作业的有状态作业队列而苦苦挣扎。 我们必须配置 Redis，配置我们的队列，设置长寿命的工作人员，然后自己处理可观察性、容量和故障。

为什么这很重要？ 您需要后台作业才能拥有可靠且快速的系统。 但...

后台工作并不现代，它让我们的生活变得比需要的更艰难。

在这篇文章中，我们将讨论现代工作系统是什么样的，你将如何使用它，然后展示一个已经实现所有东西的库😎

基于 TypeScript 的现代作业队列会是什么样子？

  • 真正的无服务器：我们不应该配置 redis、服务器、容量等。
  • 零配置：我们不想在需要之前配置每个队列。
  • 扇出（事件驱动） ：您应该能够从一次调用中触发 > 1 个作业
  • 全类型：您应该始终知道具有全类型的每个函数可用的数据
  • 可靠：失败应该自动重试
  • 能够处理复杂性：工作很复杂，您应该能够为并行性和可靠性指定多个单独步骤的函数
  • 厨房水槽：一个好的平台还应该为您处理幂等性、节流、退避和缩放等问题

世界已经变得无服务器。 我们不必担心扩展、故障和有状态的服务器。 我们也不必担心配置队列或作业失败，因为您推送到队列中的数据无效。 而且我们应该能够同时运行多个函数！

除了无服务器，秘诀是：作业队列应该是事件驱动的。 当您发送这些事件时，相关功能就会运行。 简单的！ 这也应该是后台作业的工作方式。

设计 API

创建无服务器后台作业应该很简单，零设置：

 import { createFunction } from " inngest " ; export default createFunction ( // Function name, for observability, logs, and metrics " Send welcome email " , // Event name that runs the function " app/user.created " , // The function code ({ event }) => { sendEmailTo ( event . data . id , " Welcome! " ); } );

运行它们应该很容易：

 // Send events import { Inngest } from " inngest " ; const inngest = new Inngest ({ name : " My App " }); // Run the function above automatically, in the background inngest . send ( " app/user.created " , { data : { id : 123 } });

事件在后台自动并行触发任意数量的功能。 这解决了我们所有的问题。 我们可以使用任何框架（如 NextJS）来构建部署到 Vercel、Netlify 或 Cloudflare 的无服务器函数，然后运行可靠的后台函数来执行您需要的任何操作：

  • 发送电子邮件
  • 调用外部 API
  • 按计划运行函数以生成报告
  • 核心 API 中不应该发生的任何事情，以使您的 API 更快、更可靠

您无需在AWS上设置 Redis、配置队列、设置 SQS 或 SNS 即可立即使用此功能。

我们今天发布了我们的 SDK，允许您在任何 javascript 或 typescript 项目中免费执行此操作： https ://github.com/inngest/inngest-js。 您可以在此处阅读文档： https ://www.inngest.com/docs

我很想分解它是如何在幕后工作的，这篇文章已经很长了。 如果您想了解架构和系统设计，请告诉我您的想法！

原文:

We’re closer to Jan 2023 than 2022. Serverless has taken over our stack, but we’re still struggling with stateful job queues for background work & scheduled jobs. We have to configure Redis, configure our queues, set up long-lived workers, and then handle observability, capacity, and failures ourself.

Why is this important? You need background jobs to have a reliable and fast system. But...

Background jobs aren’t modern, and it’s making our lives harder than it needs to be.

In this post, we’ll talk about what a modern job system looks like, how you’d use it, and then show a library that implements everything already 😎

What would a modern TypeScript based job queue look like?

  • Truly serverless: we shouldn’t have to configure redis, servers, capacity, etc.
  • Zero config: we don’t want to have to configure each queue before we need it.
  • Fan out (event driven): you should be able to trigger > 1 job from a single call
  • Fully typed: you should always know the data available to each function with full types
  • Reliable: failures should be retried automatically
  • Able to handle complexity: jobs are complex, and you should be able to specify a function as many individual steps for parallelism and reliability
  • Kitchen sink: a good platform should also handle things like idempotency, throttling, backoffs, and scaling for you

The world has gone serverless. We shouldn’t have to worry about scaling, failures, and stateful servers. We shouldn’t also have to worry about configuring queues, or jobs failing because the data you pushed onto a queue was invalid. And we should be able to run more than one function at once!

Alongside serverless, the secret sauce is: job queues should be event-driven. Node has an event emitter built in, and here’s the basics: you write functions that listen for specific events. When you send those events, the relevant functions run. Simple! This should be the way background jobs work, too.

Designing the API

Creating serverless background jobs should be simple, with zero setup:

import { createFunction } from "inngest"; export default createFunction( // Function name, for observability, logs, and metrics "Send welcome email", // Event name that runs the function "app/user.created", // The function code ({ event }) => { sendEmailTo(event.data.id, "Welcome!"); }
);

And running them should be easy:

// Send events
import { Inngest } from "inngest";
const inngest = new Inngest({ name: "My App" }); // Run the function above automatically, in the background
inngest.send("app/user.created", { data: { id: 123 } });

Events trigger any number of functions automatically, in parallel, in the background. Inngest also stores a history of all events for observability, testing, and replay.

This solves all of our problems. We can use any framework (like NextJS) to build serverless functions that deploy to Vercel, Netlify, or Cloudflare, then run reliable background functions that do anything you need:

  • Sending emails
  • Calling external APIs
  • Run functions on a schedule to generate reports
  • Anything that shouldn't happen in your core API to make your API faster and more reliable

You should never have to set up Redis, configure queues, set up SQS or SNS on AWS to get this functionality out of the box.

We've released our SDK that allows you to do this in any javascript or typescript project today, for free: https://github.com/inngest/inngest-js. You can read the docs here: https://www.inngest.com/docs

I'd love to break down how this works under the hood and this post is already quite long. Let me know your thoughts and if you want to see the architecture and system design!

Items.Title: 为国家选择合适的地方 | Choosing the right place for state
Items.Description:
这是我通过 Flatiron School 的软件开发计划跟踪我的进度的系列文章中的第二篇。 当我完成专注于 REACT 的第 2 阶段时，我经常纠结于何时何地使用状态。

一旦我开始处理我的项目，如何充分利用状态来确保我的组件正确渲染变得更加清晰。 稍后会详细介绍。

首先，让我们回顾一些一般准则。 那么，我们什么时候应该使用状态呢？ 以下是我们可以问自己的一些问题：

  • 它是通过道具从父母那里传下来的吗？
  • 它会随着时间的推移保持不变吗？
  • 您可以根据组件中的任何其他状态或道具来计算它吗？

如果答案是肯定的，那么我们可能不需要使用 state。

一个容易记住的规则是，状态仅适用于在组件生命周期中预期会发生变化的值。 换句话说，如果我们需要重新渲染一个组件，那么我们需要确保我们正在使用新值设置状态。 例如，重新渲染一个按钮，使其文本根据 state 的值发生变化。

现在，让我们谈谈在哪里放置状态。 我们可以问自己一些问题：

  • 哪些组件需要访问该状态？
  • 这些组件是否存在共同的组件？ 如果这个问题的答案是肯定的，那么该组件可能应该是该状态的所有者。 如果没有公共组件，则可以在层次结构中进一步创建一个组件，其唯一目的是保持该状态。

一旦我们确定了将保持状态的组件，我们将能够以 props 的形式将信息向下传递给子组件，并通过回调函数从子组件向上传递。

好的，回到我的项目：一个用于搜索、保存和提交书籍的单页应用程序。 为了实现我计划的功能，我以几种不同的方式使用状态：

  1. 它用于我的初始 GET 请求以及通过 POST 请求添加书籍或删除请求以从数组中删除书籍对数组的任何后续更改。 我使用 .map 方法为数组中的每本书呈现一个 BookCard 组件。
 const App = () => { const url = 'http://localhost:3000/books' const [books, setBooks] = useState([]) const [search, setSearch] = useState("") const [savedStatus, setSavedStatus] = useState(false) useEffect(() => { fetch(url) .then((r) => r.json()) .then((data) => setBooks(data)) }, []); const handleDeleteBook = (id) => { const updatedBooks = books.filter((book) => book.id !== id) setBooks(updatedBooks) } const handleBookAdd = (newBook) => { setBooks([...books, newBook]) }
  1. 所以 NavBar 设置我的“搜索”状态并通过回调将其发送到上游应用程序，然后“搜索”作为道具传递给它的子组件。 这意味着“搜索”将始终反映输入到搜索输入中的任何内容。 然后它用于过滤“发现”页面和“我的图书馆”页面中包含的组件中的“书籍”数组。
 const BookList = ({ books, search, onDeleteBook, onSavedBook, onUnsaveBook }) => { const unfilteredBooks = books.slice(0, 20).map((book) => <BookCard key={book.id} book={book} onDeleteBook={onDeleteBook} onSavedBook={onSavedBook} onUnsaveBook={onUnsaveBook} />) const filteredBooks = books.filter((book) => book.title.toLowerCase().includes(search.toLowerCase()) || book.author.toLowerCase().includes(search.toLowerCase())) const myFilteredBooks = filteredBooks.map((book) => <BookCard key={book.id} book={book} onDeleteBook={onDeleteBook} onSavedBook={onSavedBook} onUnsaveBook={onUnsaveBook} />) return ( <Container sx={{ display: "flex", flexWrap: "wrap", justifyContent: "left" }}> {search === "" ? unfilteredBooks : myFilteredBooks} </Container> ) }
  1. 后端更改在卡片组件内的一个函数中处理，然后作为回调一直向上传递到最顶层组件 App。 这样，每当我单击该按钮时，它都会重新呈现以反映图书“已保存”属性的正确值。

应用组件

 const handleBookSave = () => { setSavedStatus((savedStatus) => !savedStatus) } const handleUnsaveBook = () => { setSavedStatus((savedStatus) => !savedStatus) }

这里最大的挑战是确保我的组件在正确的时刻正确更新和重新渲染。 例如，我最初将我的“已保存”状态存储在卡片组件中，这导致“已保存”按钮仅在访问不同页面后刷新页面时更新。

我花了一段时间来弄清楚如何在我的应用程序中使用状态，我很高兴地说我现在有了更好的理解。 基本上，只有在需要时才使用状态。 希望这对您有所帮助，并感谢您的阅读！

原文:

This is my second of a series of posts tracking my progress through Flatiron School's Software Development program. As I worked through Phase 2, which focused on REACT, I often struggled with figuring out when and where to use state.

Once I began working on my project, it became clearer how to make the most of state to ensure my components were rendering correctly. More on that in a moment.

First, let's go over some general guidelines. So, when should we use state? Here are some questions we can ask ourselves:

  • Is it passed down from a parent via props?
  • Does it remain unchanged over time?
  • Can you compute it based on any other state or props in your component?

If the answer is yes, then we probably won't need to use state.

One easy rule to remember is that state is only for values that are expected to change during a component's life. In other words, if we need a component to re-render, then we'll want to make sure that we're setting state with a new value. For example, re-rendering a button so that its text changes based on the value of state.

Now, let's talk about where to place state. Some questions we can ask ourselves:

  • Which components need to access that state?
  • Is there a common component under which these components live? If the answer to this question is yes, then that component should probably be the owner of that state. If there isn't a common component, one can be created further up in the hierarchy with the sole purpose of holding that state.

Once we've determined the component that will hold state, we will be able to pass information down to the children components in the form of props and up from the children components via a callback function.

Ok, back to my project: a single page app to search, save, and submit books. In order to achieve the functionality I had planned, I made use of state in a few different ways:

  1. "books" - This "books" state lives in my top-most component, App, because its data will be needed across its child components housed in my "Discover" and "My Library" pages. It is used for my initial GET request and for any subsequent changes to the array either via a POST request to add book or DELETE request to remove a book from the array. I use the .map method to render a BookCard component for each book in the array. 
const App = () => {
  const url = 'http://localhost:3000/books'
  const [books, setBooks] = useState([])
  const [search, setSearch] = useState("")
  const [savedStatus, setSavedStatus] = useState(false)

  useEffect(() => {
    fetch(url)
    .then((r) => r.json())
    .then((data) => setBooks(data))
  }, []);

  const handleDeleteBook = (id) => {
    const updatedBooks = books.filter((book) => book.id !== id)
    setBooks(updatedBooks)
  }

  const handleBookAdd = (newBook) => {
    setBooks([...books, newBook])
  }
  1. "search" - My App has a NavBar component which contains a search bar with a controlled input. So NavBar sets my "search" state and sends it upstream to App via a callback, then "search" gets passed down to its child components as a prop. This means "search" will always reflect whatever is being typed into the search input. It is then used to filter the "books" array in the components contained within the "Discover" page and "My Library" page. 
const BookList = ({ books, search, onDeleteBook, onSavedBook, onUnsaveBook }) => {

    const unfilteredBooks = books.slice(0, 20).map((book) => <BookCard key={book.id} book={book} onDeleteBook={onDeleteBook} onSavedBook={onSavedBook} onUnsaveBook={onUnsaveBook} />)
    const filteredBooks = books.filter((book) => book.title.toLowerCase().includes(search.toLowerCase()) || book.author.toLowerCase().includes(search.toLowerCase()))
    const myFilteredBooks = filteredBooks.map((book) => <BookCard key={book.id} book={book} onDeleteBook={onDeleteBook} onSavedBook={onSavedBook} onUnsaveBook={onUnsaveBook} />)

    return (
        <Container sx={{ display: "flex", flexWrap: "wrap", justifyContent: "left" }}>
            {search === "" ? unfilteredBooks : myFilteredBooks}
        </Container>
    )
}
  1. "saved" - Lastly, each BookCard component has a "Save" button, which onClick updates that book's "saved" property in the backend. The backend change is handled in a function within the card component, which is then passed upstream all the way up the top-most component, App, as a callback. This way, the button re-renders to reflect the correct value of the book's "saved" property any time I click it.

BookCard component

    const handleSaveClick = () => {
      book.saved = true
      onSavedBook(book)
      console.log(book)
    }

    const handleUnsaveClick = () => {
      book.saved = false
      onUnsaveBook(book.id)
      console.log(book)
    }

App component

  const handleBookSave = () => {
    setSavedStatus((savedStatus) => !savedStatus)

  }

  const handleUnsaveBook = () => {
    setSavedStatus((savedStatus) => !savedStatus)
  }

The biggest challenge here was making sure that my components were updating and re-rendering correctly and at the correct moment. For example, I initially stored my "saved" state within the card component, which resulted in the "saved" button only updating when the page refreshed after visiting a different page.

I took me a while to figure out how state could be used within my App and I'm happy to say that I have a much better understanding now. Basically, only use state if you need it. :)

Hopefully you'll find this to be helpful and thanks for reading!

Items.Title: JavaScript 不能做什么？ | What Can't JavaScript Do?
Items.Description:
什么是 HTML 万物？

该播客涉及 Web 开发主题以及经营小型企业、个体经营和时间管理。 您可以加入他们的成功和他们的斗争，因为他们试图管理扩展他们的 Web 开发业务，而不会把自己拉得太瘦。

这是关于什么的？

本周 Mike 和 Matt 讨论了 JavaScript 的许多用例。 在过去的几年里，JavaScript 的流行度一直在稳步爆炸式增长，适当数量的框架和工具随着大家的兴趣一起发布。 尽管如此，有什么是 JavaScript 不能做的吗？ 它擅长什么？

话题

创建交互式网络应用程序
  • 创建交互式用户体验
  • 单线程服务器处理
  • 跨平台编译
    • 可以用 JavaScript 编写并跨桌面和移动应用程序运行的简单应用程序创建
  • 使用/学习 javascript 的其他原因
    • 最受欢迎的语言之一
    • 由于网络应用程序提供的视觉指标，学习很有趣

    • 结构
    • 因为 JavaScript 很灵活，所以在开发应用程序时没有一个简单的结构可以遵循（如 MVC）
    • 框架使这变得复杂，因为它们添加了自己的范例
  • 高可用性/可靠性
    • 因为 JavaScript 是一种运行时脚本语言，所以它不会编译成机器代码。 这可能会导致垃圾收集和内存使用等意外问题
  • 一致性
    • 由于 JavaScript 依赖于许多不同的引擎（V8、webkit 等），因此它们之间存在不一致
    • 一个应用程序可以在不同的浏览器和平台上以不同的方式和意外地执行
  • 速度
    • 如果性能和速度是一个严重的问题，那么 JavaScript 可能不是适合您的语言
    • 由于它是一种高级语言，它的性能不如 C 或 Rust（低级语言）
  • 需要快速、24/7 运行和一致的实时系统正是您应该倾向于较低级别语言的地方
  • 高可用性后端服务也使您可能希望倾向于更原生的后端语言

    • 你可以用 php、python、flutter-web (dart) 做一些事情

    后端 - PHP、Python、GO

    移动端 - Flutter、Swift、Kotlin

    桌面 - C#、斯威夫特

    嵌入式系统 - C、Rust

    What is HTML All The Things?

    HTML All The Things is a web development podcast and discord community which was started by Matt and Mike, developers based in Ontario, Canada.

    The podcast speaks to web development topics as well as running a small business, self-employment and time management. You can join them for both their successes and their struggles as they try to manage expanding their Web Development business without stretching themselves too thin.

    What's This One About?

    This week Mike and Matt discussed the many use cases for JavaScript. Over the past several years, JavaScript has been steadily exploding in popularity, with an appropriate number of frameworks and tooling being released alongside all the interest. With that though, is there anything that JavaScript can't do? What about what it excels at?

    Topics

    What is JavaScript good at?

    • Creating interactive web apps
    • Creating interactive User experiences
    • Single-threaded server handling
    • Cross-platform compiling
      • Simple app creation that can be written in JavaScript and run across desktop and mobile applications
    • 95% of apps for Frontend, backend, and mobile
    • Other reason to use/learn javascript
      • One of the most popular languages
      • Fun to learn because of the visual indicators that web apps provide

    What doesn’t JavaScript do well?

    • Structure
      • Because JavaScript is flexible it doesn’t have a straightforward structure to follow (like MVC) when developing apps
      • Frameworks complicate this as they add their own paradigms
    • High availability/reliability
      • Because JavaScript is a runtime scripting language it does not compile down to machine code. This can cause unexpected issues with things like garbage collection and memory usage
    • Consistency
      • Because JavaScript relies on many different engines (V8, webkit, etc) it has inconsistencies across them
      • One app can perform differently and unexpectedly across different browsers and platforms
    • Speed
      • If performance and speed is a serious concerns then JavaScript might not be the right language for you
      • As it is a higher-level language it does not perform as well as something like C or Rust (lower-level languages)
    • Realtime systems that need to be fast, 24/7 operation and consistent are exactly where you should lean towards a lower level language
    • High availability backend services also place you might want to lean towards a more native backend languages

    Alternatives to JavaScript

    Web - No real alternatives for frontend logic yet. You can do some stuff with php, python, flutter-web (dart)

    Backend - PHP, Python, GO

    Mobile - Flutter, Swift, Kotlin

    Desktop - C#, Swift

    Gaming - C++

    Embedded Systems - C, Rust

Items.Title: Kubernetes 可扩展性简介 | Introduction to Kubernetes extensibility
Items.Description:
它也有缺点，其中最主要的是它的复杂性和运营成本。

但是，我使用 Kubernetes 的次数越多，我就越认为它最重要的资产是可扩展性。 如果您需要平台默认不提供的东西，可以选择自己开发并集成它。 在这篇文章中，我想列出这些扩展点。

很多关于 Kubernetes 的解释都集中在架构上。 我相信他们进入了太多细节而错过了大局。 在这里，我只想强调基本概念。

在最基本的层面上，Kubernetes 只是一个能够运行容器镜像的平台。 它将其配置存储在分布式存储引擎etcd中。 此配置的最重要部分专用于对象的所需状态。 例如，您仅在使用kubectl命令行调度 pod 时更新此状态。

称为控制器的其他组件监视配置更改并读取所需的状态。 然后，他们尝试使期望状态与实际状态相协调。 这没有什么革命性的：Puppet 基于相同的控制回路方法，而 AFAIK，Chef。 通常，控制器管理单一类型的对象，例如， DeploymentController管理部署。

制作通用工具背后的想法是遵循帕累托定律：用 20% 的努力解决 80% 的问题。 不幸的是，工具越通用，用户群越广泛，定制剩余 20% 的工作量就越大。

因此，Kubernetes 提供了许多扩展点。

可扩展模型

在上面的部分中，我提到了调度一个 pod。 其他对象包括：部署、作业、服务等。

一些解决方案很容易适合这个模型。 例如，可以轻松创建三个 Hazelcast pod 的部署。 它开箱即用：Pod 将通过网络进行多播、找到彼此并形成一个集群。

其他解决方案不是那么均匀。 在KIP-500之前，Kafka 将依赖 Zookeeper。 然而，如果所有必需的组件都在同一个 pod 上，而 pod 出现故障，那它就等于没有。 我们应该将一个常规组件映射到一个 pod。

在这种情况下，我们需要一个功能齐全的 Kubernetes 清单来描述架构。 由于要求不同，我们需要使其可配置。 但两者都不能在所需的抽象级别上工作，即 Kafka 集群。

因此，Kubernetes 允许设计一个新的Kafka对象。 这种自定义对象称为CRD 。 这是一个简单的任意Foo对象的示例：

 apiVersion : apiextensions.k8s.io/v1 #1 kind : CustomResourceDefinition metadata : name : foos.frankel.ch #2 spec : group : frankel.ch #3 names : plural : foos #4 singular : foo #5 kind : Foo #6 scope : Namespaced #7 versions : - name : v1alpha1 served : true #8 storage : true #9 schema : openAPIV3Schema : type : object properties : spec : type : object properties : bar : type : string required : [ " bar" ] required : [ " spec" ]
  1. 必需的标头
  2. 匹配以下<plural>.<group>
  3. 用于 CLI 和显示的单数名称
  4. 在清单中使用
  5. 可以是Cluster或Namespaced 。 默认， default
  6. 可以启用/禁用一个版本
  7. 最新版本必须标记为存储版本

应用此清单后，您可以管理您的Foo 。 让我们创建一个清单来创建一个新的Foo对象。

 apiVersion : foos.frankel.ch/v1alpha1 kind : Foo metadata : name : myfoo spec : bar : " whatever"
 kubectl apply -f foo.yml kubectl get foo

上面的命令用新的Foo类型更新了数据模型并创建了一个Foo对象。 但实际上，我们只是通过 Kubernetes API 将数据存储在etcd中。 在我们启动一个控制器来监视新对象并对其进行操作之前，什么都不会发生。 请注意，管理 CRD 的控制器的名称是operator 。

可扩展的验证

可以运行第三方工作负载的平台的一个常见问题是只允许经过审查的工作负载。 一些工作负载可能会消耗过多的资源； 其他人可能是恶意的。

这里有两个具体的场景：

  • 作为集群操作员，您希望管理集群的有限物理资源（CPU/内存）并在所有 pod 之间共享它们。 为此，您希望强制每个 pod 描述其资源需求。 开发人员通过设置request和limits属性来实现这一点。 您想禁止没有它们的 pod。
  • 作为具有安全意识的操作员，您希望防止特权升级。 它不应该改变 pod 的最终行为。 您想将allowPrivilegeEscalation=false添加到每个 pod。

虽然可以通过“构建”管道管理这两种情况，但 Kubernetes 提供了一种开箱即用的解决方案。

正如我上面所解释的，Kubernetes 存储配置是etcd ，而控制器会监视更改并对其进行操作。 为了防止不必要的行为，最安全的方法是验证更改配置的有效负载； 这是准入控制器的角色。

准入控制器是一段代码，它在对象持久化之前截取对 Kubernetes API 服务器的请求，但在请求经过身份验证和授权之后。 控制器由以下列表组成，被编译成 kube-apiserver 二进制文件，并且只能由集群管理员配置。 在该列表中，有两个特殊的控制器： MutatingAdmissionWebhook和ValidatingAdmissionWebhook 。 它们执行在 API 中配置的变异和验证（分别）准入控制 webhook。

简而言之，有两种准入控制器可用：

  1. 验证准入 webhook 允许/阻止请求更改状态
  2. 它们按照下图依次运行：

    从Kubernetes 准入控制器指南

    每个都可以解决上面突出显示的场景。

    可扩展的客户端功能

    在最基本的层面上， kubectl命令行是对 REST 客户端的高级抽象。 您可以通过设置详细选项来验证它：

    有时，您需要运行多个命令才能获得所需的结果。 例如，我们想查询哪些主体可以执行一个动作。

    该机制与 Git 的机制非常相似：

    1. 您根据特定格式编写代码 -插件
    2. 您将其设置在PATH变量中

    从这一点上， kubectl可以发现它。

    你可以在你的机器上管理你的插件，但这种方法不能扩展到整个组织。 解决方案是插件管理器。 认识克鲁：

    克鲁帮助您：

    • 发现kubectl插件，
    • 将它们安装在您的机器上，
    • 并保持安装的插件是最新的。

    关于哪些主体可以执行一个动作，这里是如何做到的：

     brew install krew #1 kubectl krew completion #2 # follow instructions to update your shell kubectl krew update #3 kubectl krew install who-can #4 k who-can watch pod #5

    结论

    在这篇文章中，我们浏览了 Kubernetes 中的几个扩展点：数据模型、准入控制器和客户端。 这是一个非常简短的介绍，无论是宽度还是深度。 然而，我希望它为进一步的研究提供了一个很好的切入点。

原文:

Kubernetes offers a lot of benefits: an enormous ecosystem with plenty of actors, self-healing capabilities, etc. There's no free lunch, though. It also comes with downsides, chief among them its complexity and operating costs.

However, the more I work with Kubernetes, the more I think its most significant asset is extensibility. If you need something that the platform doesn't provide by default, there's an option to develop it yourself and integrate it. In this post, I'd like to list such extension points.

Kubernetes 101

A lot of explanations on Kubernetes focus on the architecture. I believe they go into too many details and miss the big picture. Here, I only want to highlight the basic concepts.

At its most basic level, Kubernetes is just a platform able to run container images. It stores its configuration in a distributed storage engine, etcd. The most significant part of this configuration is dedicated to the desired state for objects. For example, you only update this state when you schedule a pod using the kubectl command line.

Other components, called controllers, watch configuration changes and read the desired state. Then, they try to reconcile the desired state with the actual state. It's nothing revolutionary: Puppet is based on the same control-loop approach, and AFAIK, Chef. Generally, a controller manages a single type of object, e.g., the DeploymentController manages deployments.

The idea behind making a generic tool is to follow Pareto's Law: solve 80% of the problems with 20% of the effort. Unfortunately, the more generic the tool and the wider the user base, the more effort to customize the remaining 20%.

Kubernetes designers saw this issue as the most critical obstacle to widespread adoption. Hence, Kubernetes offers many extension points.

Extensible model

In the section above, I mentioned scheduling a pod. A pod is one of the many objects available in Kubernetes out-of-the-box. Other objects include: deployments, jobs, services, etc.

Some solutions easily fit this model. For example, one can easily create a deployment of three Hazelcast pods. It works out-of-the-box: the pods will multicast over the network, find each other, and form a cluster.

Other solutions are not so homogenous. Before KIP-500, Kafka would rely on Zookeeper. At cluster consists of at least three Zookeeper nodes and as many Kafka nodes as desired. Kubernetes makes it possible to deploy multiple images on the same pod. Yet, if all required components are on the same pod and the pod fails, it amounts to nothing. We should map one regular component to one pod.

In this case, we need a fully-featured Kubernetes manifest that describes the architecture. Because of different requirements, we will need to make it configurable. Kubernetes' ecosystem offers several alternatives to manage this problem: Kustomize and Helm count among the most popular solutions. But neither work at the desired level of abstraction, the Kafka cluster.

Therefore, Kubernetes allows designing a new Kafka object. This kind of custom object is known as a CRD . Here's a sample for a simplistic arbitrary Foo object:

apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1 #1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata: name: foos.frankel.ch #2
spec: group: frankel.ch #3 names: plural: foos #4 singular: foo #5 kind: Foo #6 scope: Namespaced #7 versions: - name: v1alpha1 served: true #8 storage: true #9 schema: openAPIV3Schema: type: object properties: spec: type: object properties: bar: type: string required: ["bar"] required: ["spec"]
  1. Required header
  2. Match the following <plural>.<group>
  3. Group name for REST API - /apis/<group>/<version>
  4. Plural name for the REST API - /apis/<group>/<version>/<plural>
  5. Singular name to be used on the CLI and for display
  6. Used in manifests
  7. Can be either Cluster or Namespaced. A Cluster resource is declared cluster-wide, and there can be a single one per cluster; Namespaced resources can be multiple and need to be under a namespace; by default, default
  8. A version can be enabled/disabled
  9. The latest version must be marked as the storage version

Once you've applied this manifest, you can manage your Foo. Let's create a manifest to create a new Foo object.

apiVersion: foos.frankel.ch/v1alpha1
kind: Foo
metadata: name: myfoo
spec: bar: "whatever"

kubectl apply -f foo.yml
kubectl get foo

The above commands have updated the data model with a new Foo type and created a Foo object. But under the cover, we've only stored data in etcd via the Kubernetes API. Nothing will happen until we start a controller that watches for new objects and acts upon them. Note that the name for a controller that manages CRDs is operator.

Extensible validation

A common concern with a platform that can run third-party workloads is allowing only vetted ones. Some workloads may consume too many resources; others may be malicious.

Here are two concrete scenarios:

  • As the cluster operator, you want to manage your cluster's limited physical resources (CPU/memory) and share them among all pods. For this, you want to enforce that each pod describes its resources requirements. Developers achieve this by setting the request and limits attributes. You want to disallow pods that don't have them.
  • As a security-minded operator, you want to prevent privilege escalation. It shouldn't change the final behavior of the pod. You want to add the allowPrivilegeEscalation=false to every pod.

While one can manage both cases through a "build" pipeline, Kubernetes provides a solution out-of-the-box.

As I explained above, Kubernetes stores configuration is etcd while controllers watch changes and act upon them. To prevent unwanted behavior, the safest way is to validate payloads that change configuration; it's the role of admission controllers.

An admission controller is a piece of code that intercepts requests to the Kubernetes API server prior to persistence of the object, but after the request is authenticated and authorized. The controllers consist of the list below, are compiled into the kube-apiserver binary, and may only be configured by the cluster administrator. In that list, there are two special controllers: MutatingAdmissionWebhook and ValidatingAdmissionWebhook. These execute the mutating and validating (respectively) admission control webhooks which are configured in the API.

-- Using Admission Controllers

In short, two kinds of admission controllers are available:

  1. The validating admission webhook allows/prevents a request from changing the state
  2. The mutating admission webhook changes the request

They run in turn as per the following diagram:

From A Guide to Kubernetes Admission Controllers

Each can solve the scenarios highlighted above.

Extensible client capabilities

At its most basic level, the kubectl command line is a high-level abstraction over a REST client. You can verify it by setting the verbose option:

Kubernetes' REST API is (mostly?) based on CRUD operations. Sometimes, you need to run several commands to achieve the desired results. For example, we would like to query which subjects can execute an action.

kubectl includes a mechanism to write code to orchestrate these calls. The mechanism is pretty similar to Git's:

  1. You write your code according to a specific format - a plugin
  2. You set it in your PATH variable

From this point, kubectl can discover it.

You can manage your plugins on your machine, but this approach is not scalable to a whole organization. The solution is a plugin manager. Meet Krew:

Krew is the plugin manager for kubectl command-line tool.

Krew helps you:

  • discover kubectl plugins,
  • install them on your machine,
  • and keep the installed plugins up-to-date.

-- What is Krew?

Regarding which subjects can execute an action, here's how to do it:

brew install krew #1 
kubectl krew completion #2
# follow instructions to update your shell
kubectl krew update #3
kubectl krew install who-can #4
k who-can watch pod #5
  1. Install brew on Mac
  2. Display the auto-completion instructions
  3. Update the cached list of plugins
  4. Install the who-can Krew plugin
  5. Enjoy!

Conclusion

In this post, we browsed through several extension points in Kubernetes: the data model, admission controllers, and client-side. It was a very brief introduction, both in width and depth. Yet, I hope that it gives a good entry point into further research.

养马所需物品
养马所需的材料清单非常少。要在 Minecraft 中养马，您只需要两匹马和 2 个金苹果、2 个金胡萝卜，或者各一个。 由于金苹果提供增益，您应该为自己保留金苹果并使用金胡萝卜。 更不用说金胡萝卜只需要金块而不是更昂贵的金锭。 寻找胡萝卜是定位一个村庄或耐心等待它们从僵尸身上掉落的问题。

如何在 Minecraft 中繁殖马
你必须驯服他们两个。 驯服一匹马可以通过右键单击它来完成，如果它炸了你，就往后跳，直到它停止从后面踢你。 成功驯服它们后，将它们靠近并喂它们两个金胡萝卜。

This guide will outline the required materials for horse breeding, the steps required to do so, and what you can achieve with horses in Minecraft. Required items to Breed Horses
2 Golden Carrots / 2 Golden Apple

2 Adult Horses

The list of necessary materials for horse breeding is quite small.to breed horses in Minecraft, You just need two horses and either 2 golden apples, 2 golden carrots, or one of each. Since golden apples offer buffs, you should keep the golden apples for yourself and look to use the golden carrot. Not to mention that the golden carrot only requires gold nuggets instead of the much more expensive gold ingots. Finding carrots is a matter of locating a village or patiently waiting for them to drop from zombies.

How to breed Horses in Minecraft
you have to tame both of them. Taming a horse can be done by right-clicking on it, if it blasts you, jump back up until it stops kicking you from behind. After successfully taming them both, keep them close and feed them both golden carrots.

Items.Title: 2022 年 12 个最适合编程的 Linux 发行版 | 12 Best Linux Distros For Programming in 2022
Items.Description:
它是免费和开源的。

发行版用于将运行操作系统所需的所有软件捆绑在一起，包括内核、库、实用程序和应用程序。

有许多不同的发行版（“发行版”）可用，每种都有自己的优点和缺点。 有些比其他更适合某些任务。 在本文中，我们将介绍 12 个最适合开发人员的 Linux 发行版。

对于初学者或不熟悉Linux内核的人来说，这是一个不错的选择。

基于 Debian 或 Ubuntu 的发行版（例如 Mint、Elementary 和 Zorin）对于开发人员来说也是不错的选择，因为它们易于设置和使用。 这些发行版带有大量预装的应用程序和驱动程序，非常适合那些想要立即开始编程的人。

对于更有经验的开发人员和 L​​inux 爱好者，还有一些“最小”的发行版不附带任何预安装的应用程序。 这些发行版要求用户从头开始安装和设置所有内容，这可能是一个很好的学习体验。 但是，重要的是要注意这些发行版不适合初学者。

是什么造就了一个好的 Linux 编程发行版？

在用于开发和编程的优秀 Linux 发行版中需要寻找一些关键的东西。 首先，它应该带有很好的预装编程工具、开发工具和语言选择。 其次，它应该有一个相对较短的发布周期，以便您可以随时了解最新版本的工具和语言。 最后，安装新的软件包和更新应该很容易。

如果您是程序员，如何选择最适合您的 Linux 发行版？

为您的需求选择最好的 Linux 发行版可能很棘手。 有数百个 Linux 发行版可用，每个发行版都有自己的发布周期、编程语言支持和桌面环境。

对于想要保持技术前沿的开发人员来说，Fedora 是一个不错的选择。 它的发布周期比大多数其他发行版更短，因此您将始终拥有最新最好的软件。 对于那些想要更稳定的发布周期的人来说，Ubuntu 是一个不错的选择。 如果您想使用特定的编程语言，这也是一个不错的选择，因为有许多 Ubuntu 衍生产品可以满足特定语言的需求。

最终，最适合您的 Linux 发行版是满足您特定需求的发行版。 如果您不确定这些需求是什么，请尝试几个不同的发行版，看看哪一个适合您。

为什么选择发行版很重要？

有数百个发行版可用，那么为什么选择正确的发行版很重要？ 对于开发人员来说，选择正确的发行版很重要，因为它可以在稳定性、安全性和性能方面产生重大影响。 一个非常适合开发的发行版将预装正确的工具，并且很容易安装新软件。 最前沿的发行版非常适合希望始终拥有最新最好的软件的开发人员，但它们可能更不稳定。 如果您正在寻找更稳定的开发环境，您可能希望选择比最新版本滞后几个月的发行版。 选择流行发行版时要考虑的另一个因素是包管理器。 一些包管理器比其他包管理器更擅长处理依赖关系。 如果您不确定要使用哪个包管理器，请四处询问或在线查找评论。

为什么 Linux 更适合开发人员和程序员？

出于多种原因，Linux 是程序员和开发人员的绝佳选择。 首先，它是一个开源操作系统，这意味着任何人都可以为其开发做出贡献。 其次，Linux 有许多不同的编程语言可供选择，因此您可以选择最适合您需求的一种。 第三，Linux 发行版可用于各种不同的桌面环境，因此您可以找到适合您工作流程的版本。 最后，Ubuntu 是一个流行的 Linux 发行版，它易于使用并且得到了社区的大量支持。

哪个是 2022 年编程的最佳 Linux 发行版？

这是为各种用户编程的最佳 Linux 发行版列表。

它是基于 Debian 的发行版，易于安装，并且有大量在线资源可帮助您入门。 另外，它是免费的！ 对于程序员来说，Ubuntu 长期支持 (LTS) 版本提供了一个稳定的开发环境，他们不需要每六个月升级一次。 如果您是 Linux 初学者或 Linux 生态系统的新手，那么这是您的最佳选择。 使用 Ubuntu，您将快速开始使用 Linux 命令和包管理器。 支持许多编程语言，因此，它很容易采用，是编程的好选择。


它基于 Arch Linux，具有许多相同的功能，但更易于安装和使用。 它是中级开发人员或程序员的最佳发行版之一。 它结合了 Arch Linux 的所有功能，适合寻找 Arch 替代品的初学者。 此外，Manjaro 有 GNOME、KDE ​​和 XFCE 三个版本。 它也有自己的存储库，因此您不必担心使用 Arch User Repository(AUR)。


这是一个非常轻量级的发行版，可以根据您的需要轻松定制。 如果你认为自己是一名高级程序员，或者你想要一些挑战，那就去 Arch Linux


它由 Offensive Security Ltd 维护和资助。开发团队由 600 多人组成，拥有庞大的贡献者社区。

由于其大量的安全相关工具，它是安全程序员最受欢迎的选择。 这些工具可用于测试系统的安全性并发现漏洞。 道德黑客和安全研究人员经常使用 Kali Linux 在其他系统上执行黑客攻击。 如果您正在寻找可以帮助您进行渗透和安全编程的 Linux 发行版，那就别无所求。 开发人员和安全研究人员可以通过使用 Kali Linux 作为他们选择的 Linux 操作系统获得很多好处


它还易于定制和扩展，使其成为开发和部署服务器应用程序的多功能平台。

它们都提供了广泛的选项和工具来定制您的系统。 萨巴永（图片来源：维基媒体）

是的，Ubuntu 最适合编程，因为它是一个开源操作系统，在开发人员中很受欢迎，并且支持大多数编程语言。

我怎样才能成为 Linux 专业人士？

这个问题没有万能的答案，因为成为 Linux Pro 的最佳方式可能会因您的经验和专业水平而异。 但是，有关如何成为 Linux 专业人士的一些技巧包括：定期学习和练习、参加会议和研讨会以及与其他 Linux 专业人士建立联系。 此外，及时了解 Linux 社区的最新趋势和发展以提高您的 Linux 专业知识也很重要。

为什么 Debian 如此受欢迎？

它稳定、得到良好支持，并拥有庞大的用户和开发者社区。 这个问题没有简单的答案，因为它取决于您在操作系统中寻找什么。 对于那些想要一个稳定可靠的系统的人来说，Fedora 是一个受欢迎的选择，而 Debian 通常是那些想要一个更可定制的体验的人的首选。 最终，最适合您的操作系统是满足您的需求和偏好的操作系统。

结论

原文:

Linux is a Unix-like operating system that was originally created by Linus Torvalds. Linux is typically packaged in a Linux distribution. It is free and open source.

A Linux distribution (distro) is an operating system made from a software collection, which is based upon the Linux kernel and, often, a package management system. Distributions are used to bundle together all of the necessary software to run an operating system, including the kernel, libraries, utilities and applications.

There are many different distributions (“distros”) available, each with its own benefits and drawbacks. Some are better suited for certain tasks than others. In this article, we will take a look at 12 of the best Linux distros for developers.

Gnome is a popular desktop environment for Linux that is known for being user-friendly and easy to use. It is a good choice for beginners or those who are not familiar with the Linux kernel.

Distros that are based on Debian or Ubuntu (such as Mint, Elementary, and Zorin) are also good choices for developers as they are easy to set up and use. These distros come with a large number of pre-installed applications and drivers, making them ideal for those who want to get started with programming right away.

For more experienced developers and Linux enthusiasts, there are also a number of “minimal” distros that do not come with any pre-installed applications. These distros require the user to install and set up everything from scratch, which can be a good learning experience. However, it is important to note that these distros are not suitable for beginners.

What makes a good Linux distro for programming?

There are a few key things to look for in a good Linux distro for development and programming. Firstly, it should come with a good selection of pre-installed programming tools, development tools and languages. Secondly, it should have a relatively short release cycle , so that you can always be up-to-date with the latest versions of tools and languages. Lastly, it should be easy to install new software packages and updates.

How to choose the best Linux distro for you if you are a programmer?

Choosing the best Linux distro for your needs can be tricky. There are hundreds of Linux distributions available, and each has its own release cycle, programming language support, and desktop environment.

Fedora is a good choice for developers who want to stay on the bleeding edge of technology. Its release cycle is shorter than most other distros, so you’ll always have the latest and greatest software available. Ubuntu is a good choice for those who want a more stable release cycle. It’s also a good choice if you want to use a specific programming language, as there are many Ubuntu derivatives that cater to specific languages.

Ultimately, the best Linux distro for you is the one that meets your specific needs. If you’re not sure what those needs are, try out a few different distros and see which one feels right for you.

Why Is Choosing a Distro Important?

There are hundreds of distros available, so why is it important to choose the right one? For developers, choosing the right distro is important because it can make a big difference in terms of stability, security, and performance. A distro that is well-suited for development will come with the right tools pre-installed and will be easy to install new software on. Bleeding edge distros are great for developers who want to always have the latest and greatest software, but they can be more unstable. If you’re looking for a more stable development environment, you may want to choose a distro that lags behind the latest releases by a few months. Another factor to consider when choosing a popular distro is the package manager. Some package managers are better at handling dependencies than others. If you’re not sure which package manager to use, ask around or look for reviews online.

Why Is Linux Better for Developers and Programmers?

Linux is a great choice for programmers and developers for a number of reasons. First, it is an open-source operating system, which means that anyone can contribute to its development. Second, there are many different programming languages available for Linux, so you can choose the one that best suits your needs. Third, Linux distributions are available for a variety of different desktop environments, so you can find one that fits your workflow. Finally, Ubuntu is a popular Linux distribution that is easy to use and has a lot of support from the community.

Which Is The Best Linux Distributions For Programming 2022?

This is a list of the best Linux distributions for programming for various users.

1. Ubuntu (best for beginners)

Ubuntu is a great choice for beginner programmers and Linux newbies. It is a distro based on Debian and is easy to install and has a lot of online resources to help you get started. Plus, it is free! For programmers, the Ubuntu Long Term Support (LTS) release provides a stable development environment that they don’t need to upgrade every six months. If you are a beginner Linux user or new to the Linux ecosystem, then this is the best option for you. With Ubuntu, you will be up to speed in getting started with Linux commands and package managers. Many programming languages are supported and as a result, it is easy to adopt and a good choice for programming.


(Image Credit: Ubuntu)

2. Manjaro Linux (best for intermediate programmers)

Manjaro is a great choice for intermediate programmers. It is based on Arch Linux and has a lot of the same features, but is much easier to install and use. It is one of the best distros for intermediate developers or programmers. Combining all the power of Arch Linux, it suits beginners looking for an Arch alternate. Moreover, Manjaro comes in three editions GNOME, KDE, and XFCE. It also has its own repositories, so you don’t have to worry about using Arch User Repository(AUR).


(Image Credit: Distrowatch)

3. Arch Linux (best for advanced programmers)

Arch Linux is a great choice for advanced programmers. It is a very lightweight distribution that can be easily customized to your needs. The Arch User Repository (AUR) is a great resource for finding and installing software. The Arch community is very friendly and helpful, and there is a lot of documentation available. If you identify yourself as an advanced programmer or you want some challenge then go for arch Linux


(Image Credit: Linux Config)

4. Kali Linux (best for security researchers)

Kali Linux is a Debian-derived Linux distribution designed for digital forensics and penetration testing. It is maintained and funded by Offensive Security Ltd. The development team comprises more than 600 people, with a large community of contributors.

It’s the most popular choice for security programmers due to its vast collection of security-related tools. These tools can be used to test the security of systems and find vulnerabilities. Ethical hackers and security researchers frequently use Kali Linux to perform hack exploits on other systems. If you are looking for a Linux distro that will help you in penetration and security programming, then look no further. Developers, as well as security researchers, can gain a lot of benefits by using Kali Linux as their choice of Linux operating system

5. Fedora (best for server programmers)

Fedora Workstation is a popular choice for server programmers due to its stability and wide range of software packages. It is also easy to customize and extend, making it a versatile platform for developing and deploying server applications.

6. openSUSE (best for system admins)

OpenSUSE is a great choice for system administrators and developers who want a stable and reliable operating system. It is one of the oldest Linux systems that was created in 1992. It is well-supported and comes with a wide range of tools and features that make it easy to administer a server.

7. Raspberry Pi OS(best for education purposes)

Raspberry Pi OS(previously called Raspbian) is a Linux distro that is optimized for the Raspberry Pi hardware. Like Ubuntu or Linux Mint, it is also based on Debian. It is the official operating system for the Raspberry Pi and comes with a wide range of software that is suitable for education , programming , and general use. It provides a programming environment for Raspberry Pi software development.

8. Drauger OS (best for game programmers)

Drauger OS is a great option for game programmers. It is based on Ubuntu, so it has all of the features and benefits that come with that operating system. It provides a good development platform for game programmers who are interested in Linux gaming. In addition, Drauger OS comes with a number of features specifically designed for game development, such as a custom kernel and support for Vulkan.

9. Zorin OS (best for windows based programmers)

Zorin OS is a great option for programmers coming from Windows or macOS. It’s based on Ubuntu, so it’s easy to use and familiar for anyone who’s used Windows before. Plus, it comes with all the tools you need to get started programming right away.


(Image Credit:Tecmint)

10. Gentoo/Sabayon Linux (best for customizations)

Gentooand Sabayon Linux are two of the most popular distros for customizations. They both offer a wide range of options and tools to customize your system. Gentoo is known for its flexibility and customizability, while Sabayon is known for its ease of use and user-friendly interface. Sabayon Linux is the best Linux distro for developers and system designers who want to customise the feel of their Linux operating system


Sabayon(Image Credit: Wikimedia)

11. Linux Mint (best for general purpose use)

Linux Mint is a great choice for general purpose use. It is based on Ubuntu, so it has all of the same features and benefits. It has some extra features that make it even more user-friendly. For example, it comes with pre-installed software that makes it easy to get started with using Linux.

12. Android-x86 (best for Android programmers)

Android-x86 is an open-source project that aims to bring the Android operating system to x86-based PCs and laptops. It is a great choice for Android programmers who want to develop and test their apps on a desktop or laptop. Is Ubuntu Best For Programming?

Yes, Ubuntu is best for programming because it is an open-source operating system that is popular among developers and supports most programming languages.

How can I become a Linux Pro?

There is no one-size-fits-all answer to this question, as the best way to become a Linux Pro may vary depending on your level of experience and expertise. However, some tips on how to become a Linux Pro include: studying and practising regularly , attending conferences and workshops, and networking with other Linux professionals. Additionally, it is important to stay up-to-date with the latest trends and developments in the Linux community to polish your Linux expertise.

Are there alternative Linux desktop options to consider?

Yes, there are alternative Linux desktop options to consider. For example, you could try the LXDE or Xfce desktop environments.

Why is Debian so Popular?

Debian is a very popular Linux distribution for several reasons. It is stable , well-supported , and has a large community of users and developers. Debian is also easy to install and use, and it comes with a wide range of software applications.

Which Linux distro does Linus Torvalds use?

Linus Torvalds uses Fedora as his main workstation

Is Fedora better than Debian?

There is no simple answer to this question as it depends on what you are looking for in an operating system. Fedora is a popular choice for those who want a stable and reliable system , while Debian is often preferred by those who want a more customizable experience. Ultimately, the best operating system for you is the one that meets your needs and preferences.

I prefer stability. I like my software to work well and not have any glitches. I don’t mind if it’s not the newest version, as long as it’s stable.

Conclusion

There are many different Linux distributions available, and choosing the best one for programming can be a difficult task. However, I believe that the best Linux distro for programming is Ubuntu. Ubuntu is a popular distribution that is easy to use and comes with a wide range of features. It also has a large community of users and developers who can help you if you run into any problems. Items.Title: 审查 .map() 和 .filter() | Reviewing .map() and .filter()
Items.Description:
辛迪曲

发表于我目前正在进行编码训练营的第 4 周。 我们已经开始学习 React，我觉得我忘记了本周之前学到的关于 Javascript 的一切。 我想重温的两个概念是 .map() 和 .filter()。 我将详细介绍这些功能的使用方法，希望这对像我这样的新开发人员有所帮助，或者为需要的人提供复习！

什么是.map()？

原始数组不变。

如何使用 .map() 函数

const even = [2, 4, 6]; const odd = even.map(num => num + 1); // the const odd will return [3, 5, 7] // the const even will return [2, 4, 6]

我们在第一行声明了一个名为“even”的数组。 由于 map() 创建了一个新数组，我们将分配一个名为“odd”的新常量，它将包含我们的新数组。

作为复习，该函数可以重写如下。

 const even = [2, 4, 6]; function num(n){return n + 1} const odd = even.map(num); // the const odd will return [3, 5, 7] // the const even will return [2, 4, 6]

何时使用 map 而不是 forEach

什么是.filter()？

如何使用 .filter() 函数

const numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]; const odd = numbers.filter(num => num % 2 === 1); // the const numbers will return [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6] // the const odd will return [1, 3, 5]

在上面的示例中，新数组“odd”仅返回满足当数字除以 2 时余数为 1 的条件的元素。

比较 .map() 和 .filter()

 //.filter() example const numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]; const odd = numbers.filter(num => num % 2 === 1); // the const numbers will return [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6] // the const odd will return [1, 3, 5] //.map() example const numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]; const odd = numbers.map(num => num % 2 === 1); // the const numbers will return [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6] // the const odd will return [true, false, true, false, true, false]

I am currently on my 4th week of coding bootcamp. We have moved into learning React and I felt like I forgot everything I learned about Javascript prior to this week. Two concepts I want to revisit is .map() and .filter(). I will go into detail on how these functions are used and I hope this will help new developers like me or serve as a refresher for those who need one!

What is .map()?

map() is a method used to create a brand new array from calling a function for every element in the original array. map() returns a new array that is the same length as the original array. The original array is unchanged.

How to use a .map() function 

const even = [2, 4, 6];
const odd = even.map(num => num + 1);

// the const odd will return [3, 5, 7]
// the const even will return [2, 4, 6]

We are declaring an array called "even" in the first line. Since map() creates a new array, we will assign a new const called "odd" that will contain our new array.

What is going on inside map()? num => num + 1 is the callback function that is used to go through each number in the "even" array and add 1.

As a refresher, the function can be rewritten as the following.

const even = [2, 4, 6];

function num(n){return n + 1}
const odd = even.map(num);

// the const odd will return [3, 5, 7]
// the const even will return [2, 4, 6]

When to use map instead of forEach

.map() is useful for when you need a new array returned and the original array to remain unchanged. forEach does not return anything because it is modifying the values in the original array by applying the callback function on each element.

What is .filter()?

filter() is a method that returns a new array with elements that depend on the conditions passed in the function. map() will return each element in the array while filter() will only return some.

How to use a .filter() function 

const numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6];
const odd = numbers.filter(num => num % 2 === 1);

// the const numbers will return [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
// the const odd will return [1, 3, 5]

In the example above, the new array "odd" is only returning elements that match the condition of having a remainder of 1 when the number is divided by 2.

Comparing .map() and .filter() 

//.filter() example
const numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6];
const odd = numbers.filter(num => num % 2 === 1);

// the const numbers will return [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
// the const odd will return [1, 3, 5]

//.map() example
const numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6];
const odd = numbers.map(num => num % 2 === 1);

// the const numbers will return [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
// the const odd will return [true, false, true, false, true, false]

 [root@oracle ~]# cat /home/oracle/scripts/setEnv.sh # Oracle Settings export TMP=/tmp export TMPDIR=$TMP export ORACLE_HOSTNAME=oracle.labs.local export ORACLE_UNQNAME=cdb1 export ORACLE_BASE=/u01/app/oracle export ORACLE_HOME=$ORACLE_BASE/product/19.0.0/dbhome_1 export ORA_INVENTORY=/u01/app/oraInventory export ORACLE_SID=cdb1 export PDB_NAME=pdb1 export DATA_DIR=/u02/oradata export PATH=/usr/sbin:/usr/local/bin:$PATH export PATH=$ORACLE_HOME/bin:$PATH export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=$ORACLE_HOME/lib:/lib:/usr/lib export CLASSPATH=$ORACLE_HOME/jlib:$ORACLE_HOME/rdbms/jlib [root@oracle ~]#

第十七步：

在/home/oracle/.bash_profile末尾添加对 setEnv.sh 文件的引用

[root@oracle ~]# echo ". /home/oracle/scripts/setEnv.sh" >> /home/oracle/.bash_profile [root@oracle ~]#

第十八步：

创建可以从启动/关闭服务调用的 start_all.sh 和 stop_all.sh 脚本。 确保所有权和权限正确。

 [root@oracle ~]# cat /home/oracle/scripts/start_all.sh #!/bin/bash . /home/oracle/scripts/setEnv.sh export ORAENV_ASK=NO . oraenv export ORAENV_ASK=YES dbstart $ORACLE_HOME [root@oracle ~] [root@oracle ~]# cat /home/oracle/scripts/stop_all.sh #!/bin/bash . /home/oracle/scripts/setEnv.sh export ORAENV_ASK=NO . oraenv export ORAENV_ASK=YES dbshut $ORACLE_HOME [root@oracle ~]# [root@oracle ~]# chown -R oracle:oinstall /home/oracle/scripts [root@oracle ~]# chmod u+x /home/oracle/scripts/*.sh [root@oracle ~]#

这些脚本将允许我们创建 Linux 服务来自动启动/停止数据库。

现在我们可以安装 Oracle DB 软件了。

第十九步：

以oracle用户身份登录到创建的 Linux 机器并创建目录以放置 Oracle DB 安装文件。

 [root@oracle ~]# su - oracle [oracle@oracle ~]$ mkdir ora_software [oracle@oracle ~]$ pwd /home/oracle [oracle@oracle ~]$

第 20 步：

从 Oracle 支持站点下载 Oracle DB 软件并将它们放在我们刚刚安装的 Linux 服务器上。

第 21 步：

解压软件。

 [oracle@oracle ora_software]$ cd $ORACLE_HOME [oracle@oracle dbhome_1]$ unzip -oq /home/oracle/ora_software/LINUX.X64_193000_db_home.zip The unzip activity will take some time.

第 22 步：

伪造 Oracle Linux 7 需要以下导出命令。

 [oracle@oracle dbhome_1]$ export CV_ASSUME_DISTID=OEL7.6 [oracle@oracle dbhome_1]$

第 23 步：

运行交互模式：

在本指南中，我们将以静默模式运行安装程序。

 ./runInstaller -ignorePrereq -waitforcompletion -silent \ -responseFile ${ORACLE_HOME}/install/response/db_install.rsp \ oracle.install.option=INSTALL_DB_SWONLY \ ORACLE_HOSTNAME=${ORACLE_HOSTNAME} \ UNIX_GROUP_NAME=oinstall \ INVENTORY_LOCATION=${ORA_INVENTORY} \ SELECTED_LANGUAGES=en,en_GB \ ORACLE_HOME=${ORACLE_HOME} \ ORACLE_BASE=${ORACLE_BASE} \ oracle.install.db.InstallEdition=EE \ oracle.install.db.OSDBA_GROUP=dba \ oracle.install.db.OSBACKUPDBA_GROUP=dba \ oracle.install.db.OSDGDBA_GROUP=dba \ oracle.install.db.OSKMDBA_GROUP=dba \ oracle.install.db.OSRACDBA_GROUP=dba \ SECURITY_UPDATES_VIA_MYORACLESUPPORT=false \ DECLINE_SECURITY_UPDATES=true

安装需要一些时间。

第 24 步：

按照安装程序的建议，以 root 用户身份登录并运行以下命令：

 [oracle@oracle dbhome_1]$ su - Password: [root@oracle ~]# /u01/app/oraInventory/orainstRoot.sh Changing permissions of /u01/app/oraInventory. Adding read,write permissions for group. Removing read,write,execute permissions for world. Changing groupname of /u01/app/oraInventory to oinstall. The execution of the script is complete. [root@oracle ~]# /u01/app/oracle/product/19.0.0/dbhome_1/root.sh Check /u01/app/oracle/product/19.0.0/dbhome_1/install/root_oracle.labs.local_2021-03-27_02-34-02-102394345.log for the output of root script [root@oracle ~]#

现在 Oracle 软件已安装。

我们已准备好创建一个 Oracle 数据库。

第 25 步：

在创建数据库之前，我们应该启动 Oracle 监听器：

(DESCRIPTION=(ADDRESS=(PROTOCOL=tcp)(HOST=oracle.labs.local)(PORT=1521))) The listener supports no services The command completed successfully [oracle@oracle ~]$

第 26 步：

可以使用数据库配置帮助 (DBCA) 创建数据库。 它可以在交互模式或静音模式下完成。

要以交互模式创建数据库，可以运行命令：

在本指南中，我们将使用静默模式方法创建数据库。

 dbca -silent -createDatabase \ -templateName General_Purpose.dbc \ -gdbname ${ORACLE_SID} -sid ${ORACLE_SID} -responseFile NO_VALUE \ -characterSet AL32UTF8 \ -sysPassword SysPassword1 \ -systemPassword SysPassword1 \ -createAsContainerDatabase true \ -numberOfPDBs 1 \ -pdbName ${PDB_NAME} \ -pdbAdminPassword PdbPassword1 \ -databaseType MULTIPURPOSE \ -memoryMgmtType auto_sga \ -totalMemory 2000 \ -storageType FS \ -datafileDestination "${DATA_DIR}" \ -redoLogFileSize 50 \ -emConfiguration NONE \ -ignorePreReqs

 Edit /etc/oratab file as following: [oracle@oracle ~]$ cat /etc/oratab cdb1:/u01/app/oracle/product/19.0.0/db_1:Y [oracle@oracle ~]$

第 28 步：

启用 Oracle 托管文件 (OMF) 并确保 PDB 在实例启动时启动。

Configure Network as following:

Set hostname of this machine:

Set hostname of this machine:

Items.Title: 第 2 天：js 工具链热潮 | Day 2: the js toolchain craze
Items.Description:
我想将一个小型库发布为 npm 包。 它基本上只是几百行打字稿，没什么大不了的。 代码就在那里，我只需要把它包起来，对吧？ 好吧，我又天真了。 不知何故，我过去使用的所有编程语言都有一个非常精确和清晰的方式来使用、构建和共享包，但不知何故，在 JS 社区中并非如此。

我对有多少不同的“构建工具”感到困惑。 我认为自从我上次使用 gulp、grunt 和 webpack 以及其他各种构建工具（甚至是“gobble”之类的工具）以来事情已经解决了，但情况似乎变得更糟了。 现在，yarn 和 pnpm 正在影响 npm，而在构建方面，你有 esbuild、vite、snowpack、neutrino、parcel 和 bla bla bla。 然后在上面放一些“monorepo”工具，比如 lerna 和 turborepo 以及......天哪。 这并不容易。 它也与更“传统”的编程语言完全相反。 但它变得更糟，我在想。 所以我去了打字稿官方网站，看看他们是如何最小化东西的，然后繁荣！ 他们没有这个标准！ 您有一张票#8希望自 2014 年以来内置此功能，但他们集成了整个第三方工具（链）列表！

哎呀，我只是想最小化它！ 最后，我最终学习、衡量和评估了无数种不同的工具来做类似的事情。 好吧，让我们看看明年的比赛会如何发展。 作为第一次尝试，我尝试了“Parcel”，它被誉为适合我的案例的“零配置构建工具”，但生成的缩小脚本似乎远未达到最佳最小化。

明天我将尝试将 esbuild 与 typescript 结合使用，希望能成功（是的，我正在查看你的源图）。

今天的工作效率并不高。 我也被一些用作 UI 基石的库所牵制：

但它错过了一些非常基本的东西。 以旋转木马为例。 所以最后，我为它做了一个 PR： https ://github.com/cdmoro/bootstrap-vue-3/pull/691

这不是什么大问题，但仍然需要相当长的时间，这也是因为您花时间查看各种资源和工具。 还有一些我本来会做的事情，但我停在那里以避免在兔子洞里走得太远。 毕竟，这种东西都是无底洞。



原文:

I wanted to publish a small library as npm package. It's basically just a few hundred lines of typescript, nothing big. The code was there, and I just have to wrap it up, right? Well, I was naive again. Somehow, all the programming languages I used in the past had a very precise and clear way how to use, build and share packages, but somehow it's not the case in the JS community.

I was baffled by how many different "build tools" there are. I thought things had settled since last time I used gulp and grunt and webpack and other various build tools (even something like "gobble") but it seems the situation is gotten worse. Now yarn and pnpm are shadowing npm while on the build side you have esbuild, vite, snowpack, neutrino, parcel and bla bla bla. Then put on it some "monorepo" tools like lerna and turborepo and ...holy shit. It's not making things easy. It's also quite the polar opposite of more "traditional" programming languages. Java has maven, Python has pip, X has ... but javascript is like a bazaar with toolchains popping up like fashion trends.

But it gets worse, I was thinking. "Ok, let's go with the flow, let's pick the 'default' toolchain for a typescript lib." So I went to the typescript official site, to see how they minimize stuff and such, and boom! They don't have a standard for that! You have a ticket #8 wishing for having this feature built-in since 2014 but instead they have integrations for a whole list of third party tool(chain)s!

...at the end of the day, I'm still a bit unsure which one I should pick. Heck, I just want to minimize it! In the end, I ended up learning, gauging and assessing a gazillion of different tools doing similar things. Well, let's see how the race evolves next year. ;)

As a first attempt, I tried 'Parcel' which was hailed as the "zero configuration build tool" which fitted my case, but the resulting minified script seemed far from optimally minimized.

Tomorow I'll try using esbuild in combination with typescript a shot, let's hope that does the trick (yes, I'm looking at you sourcemap).

Today was not really productive. ...more annoying.

I was also sidetracked by some library I use as a cornerstone of my UI:

But it misses some really basic things. Like a carousel for example. So in the end, I got sidetracked making a PR for it: https://github.com/cdmoro/bootstrap-vue-3/pull/691

It's not something huge, but it still takes quite some time, also because you spend time looking around the various sources and tools. There are also things I would have done otherwise, but I stopped there to avoid going too much down the rabbit hole. After all, these kind of things are bottomless pits.

Items.Description:
维克多·约瑟夫

发表于所以我解决了这个 leetcode 问题，可以在这里找到。

我的思考过程很简单。 我初始化了一个计数器变量（称为'a'），它将索引整个数组（从零开始），然后我在计算每个变量的同时遍历数组。 然后我检查变量的计数是否大于 1。如果是，则删除该特定变量，如果否，则增加计数器变量以移动到下一个索引。
在一天结束时，剩下的是计数等于 1 的数字。

原文:

Victor Joseph

Posted on Sep 21 

class Solution:
    def removeDuplicates(self, nums: List[int]) -> int:
        a = 0
        for i in range(len(nums)):
            if nums.count(nums[a])>1:
                del nums[a]
            else:
                a+=1

So i solved this leetcode problem which can be found here.

My thought process was simple. I initialized a counter variable (called 'a') that would index the entire array (starting from zero) then i looped through the array while counting each variable. I then check if the count of the variable is greater than 1. If yes, then i delete that particular variable and if no, then i increment the counter variable to move to the next index.
At the end of the day, what is left are numbers whose count are equal to 1.

Hope you enjoyed reading, bye :)
